name: What type of siding holds up best in the foggy, salty air around Pacifica and Half Moon Bay?
headline: What type of siding holds up best in the foggy, salty air around Pacifica and Half Moon Bay?

mainEntity.name: What type of siding holds up best in the foggy, salty air around Pacifica and Half Moon Bay?
mainEntity.text: What type of siding holds up best in the foggy, salty air around Pacifica and Half Moon Bay?

acceptedAnswer.text: For the coastside (Pacifica, Daly City, Half Moon Bay, etc.), the best siding is usually **fiber‑cement, engineered wood, or high‑quality vinyl** made for coastal or high‑moisture climates. Fiber‑cement (like Hardie) is very popular near the ocean because it does not rot, is fire‑resistant, and handles salty air better than most wood. It’s heavier and harder to cut, so installation labor can cost more, but you get long life and a painted look without constant repainting. Engineered wood siding is lighter, looks like real wood, and is easier to work with. It still needs careful sealing, painting, and maintenance, but it usually holds up better than natural wood in damp foggy areas. Coastal‑grade vinyl can work if you choose a product rated for high UV and wind, and make sure it’s installed over a good water‑resistive barrier. It won’t rot, but it can crack or warp if it’s cheap or installed too tight. True wood siding looks great but is risky right on the coast: you need very good flashing, venting, and regular painting or staining, or it can rot and grow mold. Whatever you pick, ask about: - Wind rating and warranty for coastal zones - Corrosion‑resistant fasteners - Proper flashing and housewrap Skipping these details is a common and expensive mistake in this area’s wet, salty air.
